title: Holonomic Approximation through Convex Integration
speaker: Mélanie Theilliere
abstract: Convex integration and the holonomic approximation theorem are two well-known pillars of flexibility in differential topology and geometry. They each seem to have their own flavor and scope. The goal of this talk is to bring new perspective on this topic. We will explain how to prove the holonomic approximation theorem for first order jets using convex integration. More precisely we first prove that this theorem can easily be reduced to proving flexibility of some specific relation.
tags: Workshop on the h-principle and beyond

proof_stages.label: One-jet and holonomic-section framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up one-jets, sections of the one-jet bundle, differential constraints, and the central formal-versus-holonomic problem: when can a formal section satisfying a constraint be replaced by a holonomic jet satisfying it?

proof_stages.label: Formal solutions and convex-integration iteration
proof_stages.summary: A differential relation is defined as a subset of the one-jet space, and convex integration is described as an iterative process replacing formal derivative components one at a time until a holonomic solution is obtained.

proof_stages.label: Corrugation loops, slices, and ampleness
proof_stages.summary: The Gromov oscillatory formula is used to explain how one derivative can be altered by a loop in a slice of the relation, leading to the ampleness condition and the theorem that open ample relations satisfy the relevant h-principle.

proof_stages.label: Holonomic approximation theorem and naive non-ampleness
proof_stages.summary: The holonomic approximation theorem is stated for positive-codimension polyhedra, and the mountain-path case shows that the direct relation expressing closeness to a given section is generally not ample.

proof_stages.label: Rewriting holonomic approximation as a first-order relation
proof_stages.summary: The desired perturbation of the domain and approximating map are encoded as a single map x ↦ (delta(x),w(x)), producing a new first-order relation whose holonomic solutions recover the holonomic approximation conclusion.

proof_stages.label: Open and ample property of the rewritten relation
proof_stages.summary: The rewritten relation is shown to be open, and its slices are analyzed; for one-dimensional target the slice becomes the interior of a hyperbola, and the general target case follows componentwise, proving ampleness.

proof_stages.label: Application of convex integration to holonomic approximation
proof_stages.summary: Since the rewritten relation is open and ample, Gromov convex integration applies to produce the required holonomic approximation, with the mountain-path construction displayed as an application.

examples.name: Immersion relation and its slices
examples.note: The rank-maximal relation for immersions illustrates both what a first-order differential relation is and why the immersion relation becomes ample in codimension at least one but not in codimension zero.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes slices of the immersion relation, describing the plane-minus-line and R^3-minus-line cases and using their convex hulls to discuss ampleness

examples.name: Nash and Kuiper corrugation formulas
examples.note: The classical codimension-two Nash and codimension-one Kuiper perturbation formulas illustrate the oscillatory modifications that convex integration uses to change one derivative while keeping the map close.
examples.engagement_reason: the Nash and Kuiper corrugation formulas are displayed and described as known perturbation formulas, but no calculation or construction is carried out on a specific instance

examples.name: Mountain path holonomic approximation
examples.note: The mountain-path picture makes holonomic approximation visible and, by showing that the naive closeness relation has non-ample epsilon-ball slices, motivates the need to rewrite the problem as a different relation before applying convex integration.
examples.engagement_reason: the mountain-path example is set up with a specific section, its holonomic approximation is interpreted geometrically, its slice is computed, and later it is solved via convex integration with chosen loops/corrugations
